content.text: The Transformer encoder-decoder is the architecture that launched the modern era of deep learning for sequences: a
that reads the full source sequence in parallel, paired with an
that generates the output token by token while attending to the encoder's representations via cross-attention.
The encoder-decoder transformer, introduced in "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017), replaces RNN-based sequence-to-sequence models with pure attention. It has two major components:
: reads the entire source sequence bidirectionally and produces a set of context vectors -- one per input token
: generates the target sequence one token at a time. At each step it attends to its own previously generated tokens (masked self-attention) and to the encoder's output (cross-attention)
Both components are composed of identical

stacked N times (typically N = 6 in the original paper, up to 96 in large models). Each encoder block contains a Multi-Head Self-Attention sublayer and a Feed-Forward Network (FFN). Each decoder block adds a Cross-Attention sublayer between them.

: the encoder processes all source tokens simultaneously, and the decoder processes all target tokens simultaneously via teacher forcing, with the causal mask preventing position i from seeing position j > i.
The encoder-decoder transformer solved the core limitations of its predecessors:
: RNN seq2seq models compressed all source information into a single fixed vector -- attention lets the decoder directly inspect every encoder position
: replacing recurrence with attention enables training on modern GPU/TPU hardware at scale
: cross-attention has O(1) path length from any decoder position to any encoder position -- alignment is learned, not hard-coded
: encoder-decoder models (T5, BART, mT5) are pre-trained on massive corpora and fine-tuned with minimal data
It is the direct ancestor of T5, BART, mBART, Flan-T5, and the seq2seq backbone of many agent systems that need faithful, controllable generation from structured inputs.
: each source token attends to all other source tokens -- no directional bias
: the decoder generates until an end-of-sequence token, independent of input length
Natural separation of understanding and generation
: the encoder specializes in representation; the decoder in generation
: by conditioning the decoder on structured encoder outputs, you can enforce fidelity to the source
: translation, summarization, question generation, structured data-to-text, code generation
: the denoising pre-training objective (BART, T5) maps naturally to this structure
: an encoder-decoder has roughly twice the parameters of a decoder-only model at the same depth
: the encoder must run once over the full input before any decoding begins -- adds latency for short outputs
: cross-attention KV cache grows with encoder length; self-attention KV cache grows with decoder steps
Slower than decoder-only on generation tasks
: decoder-only GPT-style models have become dominant for open-ended generation because a single stack handles everything
: at training time the decoder sees ground-truth tokens; at inference it sees its own (possibly wrong) predictions -- leads to error accumulation
Each encoder block applies two sublayers with residual connections and layer normalization (pre-norm variant):
The FFN is a two-layer MLP with GELU or ReLU activation applied independently at each position:
Each decoder block has three sublayers:
: attends to previously generated tokens only -- causal mask ensures position i cannot see j > i
: uses decoder hidden states as queries, and encoder output as both keys and values
The key equation bridging encoder and decoder:
is projected from the decoder's current hidden state, while
are projected from the encoder output --
for all decoder steps, computed once.
are the mean and standard deviation over the feature dimension, and
are learned scale and shift parameters.

The diagram shows the two stacks: the encoder (left, blue border) with bidirectional self-attention, and the decoder (right, amber border) with causal self-attention, cross-attention (dashed blue arrow receiving K,V from encoder), and FFN. Both are repeated N times.

Attention is permutation-invariant -- it has no built-in notion of order. Positional information is injected by adding a positional encoding to the input embeddings. The original paper uses sinusoidal encoding:
, which apply relative positional biases directly to attention scores and generalize better to lengths beyond those seen during training.
: the canonical use case -- fixed-length source, variable-length target
: encoder compresses source document; decoder generates abstractive summary
: encoder reads context and question; decoder generates free-form answer
: encoder reads JSON/table; decoder narrates it in natural language
: encoder parses intent; decoder generates syntactically valid code
: encoder reads user turn and history; decoder generates response
: encoder reads observations; decoder generates structured action JSON
when you need open-ended generation or fine-tuning is impractical. Use
when source fidelity matters and the task is clearly input to output.

import math
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F


class TransformerEncoderLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, src_key_padding_mask=None):
        h = self.norm1(x)
        h, _ = self.self_attn(h, h, h, key_padding_mask=src_key_padding_mask)
        x = x + self.drop(h)
        h = self.norm2(x)
        h = self.ffn(h)
        x = x + self.drop(h)
        return x


class TransformerDecoderLayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, d_ff: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention(d_model, num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, d_ff),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(d_ff, d_model),
        )
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.norm3 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model)
        self.drop = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def forward(self, tgt, memory, tgt_mask=None, memory_key_padding_mask=None):
        # 1. Masked self-attention
        h = self.norm1(tgt)
        h, _ = self.self_attn(h, h, h, attn_mask=tgt_mask)
        tgt = tgt + self.drop(h)
        # 2. Cross-attention: Q=decoder, K=V=encoder output
        h = self.norm2(tgt)
        h, _ = self.cross_attn(h, memory, memory, key_padding_mask=memory_key_padding_mask)
        tgt = tgt + self.drop(h)
        # 3. FFN
        h = self.norm3(tgt)
        h = self.ffn(h)
        tgt = tgt + self.drop(h)
        return tgt


class MiniEncoderDecoder(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size=1000, d_model=256, num_heads=4, d_ff=1024, N=3, max_len=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.pos_enc = self._build_pos_enc(max_len, d_model)
        self.encoder = nn.ModuleList([TransformerEncoderLayer(d_model, num_heads, d_ff) for _ in range(N)])
        self.decoder = nn.ModuleList([TransformerDecoderLayer(d_model, num_heads, d_ff) for _ in range(N)])
        self.output_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)
        self.d_model = d_model

    def _build_pos_enc(self, max_len, d_model):
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, d_model)
        pos = torch.arange(max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        div = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, d_model, 2).float() * -(math.log(10000) / d_model))
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(pos * div)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(pos * div)
        return nn.Parameter(pe.unsqueeze(0), requires_grad=False)

    def encode(self, src, src_padding_mask=None):
        x = self.embed(src) * math.sqrt(self.d_model) + self.pos_enc[:, :src.size(1)]
        for layer in self.encoder:
            x = layer(x, src_padding_mask)
        return x

    def decode(self, tgt, memory, tgt_mask=None, memory_padding_mask=None):
        x = self.embed(tgt) * math.sqrt(self.d_model) + self.pos_enc[:, :tgt.size(1)]
        for layer in self.decoder:
            x = layer(x, memory, tgt_mask, memory_padding_mask)
        return self.output_proj(x)

    def forward(self, src, tgt, tgt_mask=None, src_padding_mask=None):
        memory = self.encode(src, src_padding_mask)
        return self.decode(tgt, memory, tgt_mask, src_padding_mask)


model = MiniEncoderDecoder()
src = torch.randint(0, 1000, (2, 15))
tgt = torch.randint(0, 1000, (2, 12))
T = tgt.size(1)
tgt_mask = torch.triu(torch.full((T, T), float("-inf")), diagonal=1)
logits = model(src, tgt, tgt_mask=tgt_mask)
print(f"Logits shape: {logits.shape}")

import torch


def greedy_decode(model, src, sos_id: int, eos_id: int, max_len: int = 50):
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        memory = model.encode(src)
        tgt = torch.tensor([[sos_id]])

        for _ in range(max_len):
            T = tgt.size(1)
            tgt_mask = torch.triu(
                torch.full((T, T), float("-inf")), diagonal=1
            )
            logits = model.decode(tgt, memory, tgt_mask=tgt_mask)
            next_token = logits[:, -1, :].argmax(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
            tgt = torch.cat([tgt, next_token], dim=1)
            if next_token.item() == eos_id:
                break

    return tgt[:, 1:]   # strip SOS


# Example (random weights, not trained):
model = MiniEncoderDecoder()
src = torch.randint(0, 1000, (1, 10))
seq = greedy_decode(model, src, sos_id=1, eos_id=2, max_len=20)
print(f"Generated tokens: {seq.tolist()}")

from transformers import T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer
import torch

model_name = "t5-small"
tokenizer = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(model_name)
model.eval()

article = (
    "The Amazon rainforest, covering over 5.5 million square kilometres, "
    "is the world's largest tropical rainforest and plays a crucial role "
    "in regulating the global climate by absorbing large amounts of CO2."
)
inputs = tokenizer(
    "summarize: " + article,
    return_tensors="pt",
    max_length=512,
    truncation=True,
)

with torch.no_grad():
    output_ids = model.generate(
        **inputs,
        max_new_tokens=64,
        num_beams=4,
        early_stopping=True,
        no_repeat_ngram_size=3,
    )

summary = tokenizer.decode(output_ids[0], skip_special_tokens=True)
print(f"Summary: {summary}")

print("\nEncoder layers:", len(model.encoder.block))
print("Decoder layers:", len(model.decoder.block))
enc_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.encoder.parameters())
dec_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.decoder.parameters())
print(f"Encoder: {enc_params:,}  |  Decoder: {dec_params:,}")

from transformers import BartForConditionalGeneration, BartTokenizer
import torch

model = BartForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    "facebook/bart-base", output_attentions=True
)
tokenizer = BartTokenizer.from_pretrained("facebook/bart-base")

src = "The dog chased the ball through the park."
tgt = "A dog ran after a ball."

enc = tokenizer(src, return_tensors="pt")
dec = tokenizer(tgt, return_tensors="pt")

with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(
        **enc,
        decoder_input_ids=dec["input_ids"],
        output_attentions=True,
    )

# cross_attentions: list of (batch, heads, tgt_len, src_len) per layer
cross_attn = out.cross_attentions
print(f"Cross-attention layers: {len(cross_attn)}")
print(f"Layer 0 shape: {cross_attn[0].shape}")

src_tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(enc["input_ids"][0])
tgt_tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(dec["input_ids"][0])
layer0_h0 = cross_attn[0][0, 0]   # (tgt_len, src_len)
for i, tok in enumerate(tgt_tokens[:4]):
    top_src = src_tokens[layer0_h0[i].argmax()]
    print(f"  tgt[{i}]='{tok}' -> src '{top_src}' (highest attn)")

from transformers import pipeline

# facebook/bart-large-cnn: encoder-decoder BART fine-tuned on CNN/DailyMail
summarizer = pipeline("summarization", model="facebook/bart-large-cnn")

article = """
NASA's Artemis program aims to return humans to the Moon by 2026.
The Space Launch System (SLS), the most powerful rocket ever built,
launched on its maiden flight in November 2022. The Orion capsule
traveled 270,000 miles from Earth. Engineers are now preparing the
crewed Artemis II mission to carry four astronauts around the Moon.
"""

result = summarizer(
    article.strip(),
    max_length=80,
    min_length=30,
    do_sample=False,
)
print(result[0]["summary_text"])

# Architecture of bart-large-cnn:
# - 12-layer encoder (bidirectional) + 12-layer decoder (causal + cross-attn)
# - 16 attention heads, d_model=1024
# - ~406M parameters total
# Encoder processes source once; decoder runs autoregressively
: cross-attention has no temporal structure -- adding a causal mask here prevents the decoder from seeing early source tokens and silently degrades quality
: Q must come from decoder hidden state; K and V must come from encoder output -- swapping them is a frequent copy-paste error
Re-computing encoder output each decode step
: the encoder needs to run only once per input; re-running it on every generated token wastes compute proportional to output length
: forgetting to shift target tokens right by one position -- model sees the answer while predicting it, reporting artificially perfect loss
: without proper padding masks the model attends to pad tokens and computes incorrect attention weights for shorter sequences in a batch
Not applying positional encoding to decoder inputs
: the decoder embedding also needs positional information -- easy to omit when adapting encoder-only code
: always apply softmax (or log-softmax for log-probs) before interpreting decoder output as a probability distribution
: apply layer norm before attention/FFN (not after) for stable training of deep models
: use the Noam schedule (warmup_steps=4000) or a linear warmup -- transformers are sensitive to high early learning rates
: share the encoder embedding, decoder embedding, and output projection weights -- reduces parameters by ~20% with negligible quality loss
: use epsilon=0.1 -- prevents the model from becoming overconfident, improves BLEU on translation tasks
: during beam search or multi-turn inference, compute encoder output once and pass it to all decode steps
: greedy decoding is fast but beam search (n=4-8) gives meaningfully better output for translation and summarization
: for large models, re-compute activations backward through the encoder to halve activation memory
: encoder processes the full source in O(1) sequential depth -- ideal for tensor parallelism across GPUs
: the decoder is inherently sequential (each token conditions on all previous) -- batching multiple sequences amortizes the cost
: store encoder K/V projections once per input; cross-attention cost becomes O(output_len x encoder_len x d_k) per layer
: run a small draft decoder to propose tokens, verify with the full encoder-decoder in parallel -- 2-4x throughput for long outputs
: DistilBART and distilT5 distill 6-layer encoder-decoders from 12-layer teachers with ~40% fewer params and ~70% of the quality
: Facebook's seq2seq toolkit -- large-scale translation and summarization
: open-source NMT framework, widely used in production translation pipelines
: export encoder-decoder models for optimized CPU/GPU inference
: efficient serving of encoder-decoder models with PagedAttention KV cache management

The encoder-decoder transformer dominates when source fidelity is critical and input/output are structurally different sequences. For open-ended generation without a fixed source, decoder-only models are preferred for their simpler KV cache and better scaling behavior.
: malicious tokens in the source sequence can cause the decoder to ignore key information via attention suppression -- sanitize inputs for production pipelines
: the decoder can generate fluent but unfaithful text despite high cross-attention weights on the source -- evaluate faithfulness explicitly with FactCC or SummaC
: beam search amplifies high-scoring but semantically wrong sequences -- diversity penalties and no_repeat_ngram_size reduce this risk
-- without it a degenerate model can generate tokens indefinitely
: verify your padding mask correctly excludes PAD tokens from attention -- incorrect masks cause subtle accuracy regressions that are hard to catch without careful evaluation
Think of the encoder-decoder as a

. The encoder is like an expert who reads the source text and forms a deep conceptual understanding -- a set of rich annotations (the context vectors) that capture meaning at every point in the document.

The decoder is a writer who has never seen the source directly. Instead, it can ask questions (queries) of the encoder's annotations (keys and values) at every step. The cross-attention mechanism lets the writer say:
for the current word I am writing, which parts of the source are most relevant?
-- and receive a precise, weighted blend of the encoder's knowledge.
The causal mask in the decoder ensures the writer can only look at what it has already written -- not the future. This is what makes generation
: each token is generated conditioned on all previous tokens and the full source.
The encoder reads the full source bidirectionally; the decoder generates autoregressively, conditioning on both its own history and the encoder via cross-attention
-- Q from decoder, K and V from encoder output
Layer norm, residual connections, and careful initialization (warmup + pre-norm) are essential for stable training
The encoder KV cache should be computed once and reused across all decode steps -- not recomputed per token
Encoder-decoder excels at seq2seq tasks; decoder-only dominates open-ended generation -- choose based on whether source fidelity matters
T5 and BART are the dominant pre-trained encoder-decoder foundations -- prefer fine-tuning over training from scratch
Teacher forcing shifts targets by one during training; at inference use beam search or greedy decoding
Cross-attention never uses a causal mask -- the decoder needs unrestricted access to all encoder positions
